coredata.dc:title: Constructing a novel GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary heterostructure with enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xian
Yang, Huimin
Jian, Xuan
Dai, Hongyan
Song, Xiuli
Liang, Zhenhai
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary heterostructure was successfully designed and prepared by an in-situ polymerization and electrostatic self-assembly approach. The prepared GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 was used as interlayer of bipolar membrane (BPM) to promote water dissociation. Results revealed that GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst exhibits excellent photoelectrocatalytic performance for water dissociation in BPM.

Water dissociation
Bipolar membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of photoelectrocatalytic performance of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4

U1261103), National Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant no. 201601123). Authors also acknowledge the Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences for technical assistance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of the formation of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst.
Schematic diagram of the photoelectrocatalytic performance testing.

(A) XRD patterns, (B) UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, (C) photocurrent responses and (D) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of different samples; (E) Variation in pH of compartments 2 and 4. (a, a0) BPM without interlayer; (b, b0) BPM with PANI interlayer; (c, c0) BPM with g-C3N4 interlayer; (d, d0) BPM with PANI/g-C3N4 interlayer; (e, e0) BPM with GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 interlayer.

head.ce:title: Constructing a novel GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary heterostructure with enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Taiyuan College, Taiyuan 030032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary heterostructure was successfully designed and prepared by an in-situ polymerization and electrostatic self-assembly approach. The prepared GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 was used as interlayer of bipolar membrane (BPM) to promote water dissociation. Results revealed that GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst exhibits excellent photoelectrocatalytic performance for water dissociation in BPM.

ce:section.ce:para: As a visible light photocatalyst, metal-free graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted increasing interest due to its plentiful material source, excellent chemical and thermal stability [1,2]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance is far from satisfactory due to high photogenerated charger combination rate and low electrical conductivity [3,4]. Polyaniline (PANI) is a good electron donor and excellent hole acceptor [5,6]. More importantly, the identical sp2-bonded π structure and carbon network of PANI and g-C3N4 endow them the compatible materials to develop heterostructure [7,8]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively applied in photoelectrocatalysis field because of their high-performance quantum effect and large specific surface areas [9,10]. Herein, we designed and prepared GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst via in-situ polymerization and electrostatic self-assembly approach. In order to evaluate its photoelectrocatalytic performance, GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst was added to bipolar membrane (BPM) to promote water dissociation. The possible photoelectrocatalytic mechanism was discussed and proposed based on the experimental results.

Firstly, light yellow powder g-C3N4 was synthesized based on previous reports [11]. Then, as-obtained g-C3N4 was dispersed in deionized water with vigorous ultrasound for 16h to obtain g-C3N4 nanosheet.

PANI/g-C3N4 composite was synthesized as follows: 0.05ml aniline monomer was added into a mixture of 3.0 mL HCl solution (1M) and 30ml distilled water, then added 0.085g APS and mechanically stirred for 1.0h in ice water bath. After that 0.5gg-C3N4 nanosheet was added and allowed to react for 12h, the dark green PANI/g-C3N4 composite was obtained.

Typically, 0.5g of PANI/g-C3N4 composite was added to 100mL (1mg/mL) GQDs solution, followed by sonication for 2h in a low-power ultrasonic bath. Then, the dispersion was filtrated through a 100nm membrane under continuous stirring. In order to combine the more free GQDs on the surface of PANI/g-C3N4 composite, the filtered fluid was multi-filtrated until the fluorescent signal could hardly be detected by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Finally, the filter cake was dried to obtain GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4.

The formation mechanism of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst was depicted in Fig. 1 . First, PANI/g-C3N4 composite was synthesized via π–π stacking and electrostatic attraction of positively charged PANI (the zeta potential was +37.2mV) and negatively charged g-C3N4 sheet (the zeta potential was −22.4mV). Then, GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 could be synthesized by π–π stacking and electrostatic attraction of positively charged PANI/g-C3N4 (the zeta potential was +24.5mV) and negatively charged GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photoelectrocatalytic performance of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 composite was used as interlayer of BPM to evaluate the photoelectrocatalytic performance. A BPM consists of a cation-exchange membrane (CEM, Fig. S1) and an anion exchange membrane (AEM, Fig. S2). Layer-by-layer solution casting method resulted good adhesion and firm contact between the layers (Fig. S3). Moreover, the prepared BPM has good optical transparency (Fig. S4) and water-insoluble (Fig. S5).

Photoelectrocatalytic experiments were carried out in a cell consisting five compartments separated by two pieces of BPM, CEM and AEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . 0.10M Na2SO4 solution was fed into compartment 1, 2, 4 and 5, and 0.5M NaCl was fed into compartments 3. The volume of five compartments were all 150mL, both anode and cathode were platinum plate electrodes (area 1cm2). Constant voltage across the electrodes was applied by use of a DC power supply, 350W xenon lamps served as the simulated sunlight source. GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 as the interlayer promote the water dissociation to H+ and OH−, after which these H+ ions were migrated to compartment 2 and combined with Cl− to generate HCl, simultaneously, OH− ions were migrated to compartment 4 and combined with Na+ to generate NaOH. A pH-meter was used to measure the pH changes in compartments 2 and 4 at 10min−1 intervals.

XRD patterns are presented in Fig. 3 A. PANI with broad peaks centering at around 20.6° and 25.1°, which are ascribed to periodicity parallel and perpendicular to polymer chain [12]. As for g-C3N4, PANI/g-C3N4 and GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 samples, two diffraction peaks at 27.4° and 13.3° can be observed clearly, which are indexed for graphitic materials to (002) and (100) peaks, respectively. The high-intensity peak at 27.4° originated from the characteristic interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic system [13], and weakening of diffraction peak at 13.3° is attributed to in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units [14]. The XRD peak positions and shapes of PANI/g-C3N4 and GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 composite remained unchanged, indicating that the presence of GQDs and PANI do not influence lattice structure of g-C3N4, which is very beneficial for photocatalytic properties of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4.

Optical absorptions are presented in Fig. 3B, the absorption edge of g-C3N4 sample is around 460nm, and PANI sample can not only absorb UV light, but also has strong absorption in visible light. Noticeably, PANI/g-C3N4 composite exhibits stronger absorption in the whole region of both UV and visible light, which is in agreement with the color change of the corresponding samples from light yellow to dark green (inset of Fig. 3B). Therefore, PANI can function as an effective sensitizer to enhance photocatalytic performance of composite. Moreover, there are no pronounced deviation between PANI/g-C3N4 and GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 composite, indicating that the presence of GQDs do not influence optical properties, which is very beneficial for photocatalytic properties of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 composites.

Transient photocurrents of synthesized materials are surveyed during recurrent on/off illumination cycles from a 350W xenon lamp. As shown in Fig.3C, photocurrent values decrease rapidly when the irradiation was cut off, and photocurrent comes back as soon as the lamp is turned on. Photocurrent density of PANI/g-C3N4 is much higher than both PANI and g-C3N4. This result indicates that the combination of PANI and g-C3N4 is able to restrain photogenerated charge recombination. Moreover, the presence of GQDs enhanced photocurrent density, indicating that GQDs can further reduce electron–hole recombination by improving the interfacial charge transfer. This result coincided well with the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) analysis, as seen in Fig. 3D. It clearly indicated that the diameter of the high-frequency semicircle of GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 is the smallest, implying the fastest interfacial charge transfer and the most efficient separation of photo-generated charge carriers [15,16].
Microscopic morphologies and structural information were examined using SEM (Fig. S6), TEM (Fig. S7) and FT-IR (Fig. S8) analysis.

Photoelectrocatalytic water dissociation capabilities are presented in Fig. 3E. The pH in compartment 2 decreased with time and, simultaneously, the pH increased in compartment 4. This result confirmed that the water dissociation occurred in interlayer of BPM. The pH variations in compartments 2 and 4 were morenoticeable by using the PANI/g-C3N4 interlayer than using PANI or g-C3N4 interlayer. This can be explained by the separation of electron–hole pairs. According to literature [17], the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of g-C3N4 are −0.99eV and 1.71eV (vs. NHE), while the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of PANI are 0.8eV and −1.9eV (vs. NHE) [18]. Thus, the electrons produced by PANI could easily inject into the CB of g-C3N4, and holes on the VB of g-C3N4 migrate to the π-orbital of PANI, which is very beneficial to hinder the recombination of electron–hole pairs. More importantly, the pH variations are the mostobvious by using GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 interlayer, suggesting that GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 composite have stronger photoelectrocatalytic performance in water dissociation. This finding is obtained mainly because the GQDs can further reduce electron–hole recombination by improving interfacial charge transfer. Moreover, many carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs enhance the hydrophilicity, thereby the efficiency of water dissociation was promoted.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary photocatalyst via in-situ polymerization and electrostatic self-assembly approach. Mechanistic analysis and characterization results indicate that the combination of PANI and g-C3N4 is able to restrain photogenerated charge recombination. Moreover, the presence of GQDs can further reduce electron–hole recombination by improving interfacial charge transfer. Prepared GQDs/PANI/g-C3N4 ternary composite exhibits excellent photoelectrocatalytic activity for water dissociation in BPM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work are jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Shenhua Group Corp (Grant no. U1261103), National Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant no. 201601123). Authors also acknowledge the Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences for technical assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.08.022.
